coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) mediated Z-scheme g–C3N4–CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction with enhanced visible light photocatalytic degradation of Paraben
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Maosheng
Hu, Chenyan
Yu, Junxia
Chen, Lianguo
coredata.dc:description: The construction of a novel Z-scheme system which possesses superior charge separation and high redox ability is highly desirable for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Herein, a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified g-C3N4 (GCN) and BiVO4 (BVO) composite (GCN-CQDs/BVO) was fabricated via an initial loading of CQDs on GCN, and a subsequent combination with BVO during its hydrothermal synthesis. Physical characterization (e.g. TEM, XRD, XPS) verified the intimate heterojunction structure of the composite, while CQDs improved its light absorption. The band structures of GCN and BVO were evaluated, displaying the feasibility for Z-scheme formation. In comparison with GCN, BVO, and GCN/BVO, GCN-CQDs/BVO generated the highest photocurrent and lowest charge transfer resistance, inferring the prominently improved charge separation. Under visible light irradiation, GCN-CQDs/BVO exhibited the significantly enhanced activity in degrading the typical Paraben pollutant——benzyl paraben (BzP), achieving the removal of 85.7% in 150 min. The effects of various parameters were explored, demonstrating that neutral pH was optimal, while coexisting ions (CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and humic acid impacted the degradation negatively. Meanwhile, trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique revealed that superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) were primarily responsible for BzP degradation by GCN-CQDs/BVO. In particular, with the assistance of CQDs, the generation of •O2 − and •OH was notably augmented. Based on these results, a Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism was proposed for GCN-CQDs/BVO, where CQDs acted as electron mediators to combine the holes from GCN and electrons from BVO, resulting in significantly improved charge separation and maximized redox ability. Moreover, the toxicity of BzP was remarkably reduced during the photocatalytic process, emphasizing its great potential in abating the risk of Paraben pollutants.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of GCN-CQDs/BVO. TEM images of (b) GCN and (c) BVO. (d) TEM and (e) HRTEM images of CQDs. (f) TEM image of GCN-CQDs/BVO. The top inset of (f) was the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of box 1. The bottom inset of (f) was the HRTEM image of box 2.
(a) XRD patterns of synthesized GCN, CQDs, BVO, and GCN-CQDs/BVO, and (b) is enlarged gray area in (a).

XPS spectra of GCN, CQDs, BVO, and GCN-CQDs/BVO: (a) survey; (b) C 1s and (c) N 1s of GCN, CQDs, and GCN-CQDs/BVO; (d) Bi 4f and (e) V 2p of BVO and GCN-CQDs/BVO; (f) O 1s of CQDs, BVO, and GCN-CQDs/BVO.

(a) Kubelka-Munk absorbance of the as-synthesized materials, and (b) the corresponding Tauc plots. (c) XPS valence band spectra of GCN and BVO, and (d) their schematic band gap structures. (e) Transient photocurrent responses and (f) EIS Nyquist plots of the materials.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of BzP over different catalysts under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) and (b) the corresponding first-order reaction kinetics. The effects of (c) solution pH, (d) coexisting anions (5 mM), (e) coexisting cations (5 mM), and (f) HA on BzP degradation by GCN-CQDs/BVO composite photocatalyst.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of BzP over GCN-CQDs/BVO when different trapping agents were used. (b) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of GCN-CQDs/BVO photocatalyst. (c) Kinetics of fluorescence intensity of TAOH produced from the oxidation of TA by photogenerated •OH over GCN-CQDs/BVO and GCN/BVO at 425 nm. Inset is the representative fluorescence spectral change (excitation wavelength λ = 315 nm) when irradiating the photocatalyst in 1 mM TA aqueous solution with different durations. (d) Kinetics of XTT reduction by photogenerated •O2 − over GCN-CQDs/BVO and GCN/BVO. Inset is the representative UV–Vis absorption of XTT-formazan at 476 nm. (e) A proposed mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of BzP over GCN-CQDs/BVO composite.

(a) Mortality, (b) hatching percentage, (c) malformation rate and (d) heart rate (at 96 hpf) of zebrafish embryos after exposure to BzP and its degradation solutions sampled at different time. Inset of (c) are the representive images of normally developed and deformed larval after 96 h culturing. The used photocatalyst was GCN-CQDs/BVO composite and the initial concentration of BzP was 10 mg/L. Values are represented as mean ± SEM of six replicates. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by post hoc LSD test was employed to determine the significant differences between exposure groups and control group (Milli-Q water), which was designated as *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) mediated Z-scheme g–C3N4–CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction with enhanced visible light photocatalytic degradation of Paraben

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of a novel Z-scheme system which possesses superior charge separation and high redox ability is highly desirable for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Herein, a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified g-C3N4 (GCN) and BiVO4 (BVO) composite (GCN-CQDs/BVO) was fabricated via an initial loading of CQDs on GCN, and a subsequent combination with BVO during its hydrothermal synthesis. Physical characterization (e.g. TEM, XRD, XPS) verified the intimate heterojunction structure of the composite, while CQDs improved its light absorption. The band structures of GCN and BVO were evaluated, displaying the feasibility for Z-scheme formation. In comparison with GCN, BVO, and GCN/BVO, GCN-CQDs/BVO generated the highest photocurrent and lowest charge transfer resistance, inferring the prominently improved charge separation. Under visible light irradiation, GCN-CQDs/BVO exhibited the significantly enhanced activity in degrading the typical Paraben pollutant——benzyl paraben (BzP), achieving the removal of 85.7% in 150 min. The effects of various parameters were explored, demonstrating that neutral pH was optimal, while coexisting ions (CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and humic acid impacted the degradation negatively. Meanwhile, trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique revealed that superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) were primarily responsible for BzP degradation by GCN-CQDs/BVO. In particular, with the assistance of CQDs, the generation of •O2 − and •OH was notably augmented. Based on these results, a Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism was proposed for GCN-CQDs/BVO, where CQDs acted as electron mediators to combine the holes from GCN and electrons from BVO, resulting in significantly improved charge separation and maximized redox ability. Moreover, the toxicity of BzP was remarkably reduced during the photocatalytic process, emphasizing its great potential in abating the risk of Paraben pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Parabens, a group of p-hydroxybenzoic acid esters, are widely used as preservatives in processed food, cosmetics, pharmaceutical and personal care products due to their wide-spectrum antimicrobial activities, high stability, and low cost (Wei et al., 2021). Over the past decades, the worldwide extensive consumption and inappropriate disposal of Paraben-containing products have discharged massive Parabens into the environment, resulting in severe contamination to natural rivers, ground water, tap water, and even soils (Kapelewska et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2017). For example, in a Brazilian river, the average concentrations of total Parabens were as high as 42.2 μg/L in winter and 41.5 μg/L in summer, while in spring, it was also up to 31.5 μg/L (Galinaro et al., 2022). So far, the highest environmental concentrations of Parabens were reported in surface water of Osun State, Nigeria, reaching 527, 377, 229, and 283 μg/L for the corresponding methyl-, ethyl-, propyl-, and butyl-paraben astonishingly (Bolujoko et al., 2022). Meanwhile, Parabens have been found to accumulate in various organisms (e.g. insects, shellfish, fish, marine mammals, birds) (Lu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Xue and Kannan, 2016; Xue et al., 2015) and even human body samples (e.g. urine, serum, placenta, breast milk) (Assens et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019b). However, toxicological studies suggest that Parabens have the potential to induce carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, and reproductive toxicities, especially the endocrine disrupting effect (Hu et al., 2022a; Li et al., 2020a; Liang et al., 2022; Merola et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2022). In terms of these adverse effects allied to increased occurrence in the environment, how to effectively and sustainably remove Parabens from water bodies and eliminate their ecological impact are urgent issues to be addressed.

On account of the low cost, environmental friendliness, and easy operation, photocatalytic technology driven by abundant sunlight has been recognized as one of the most promising approaches to degrade the pollutants. For such an application, the semiconductor photocatalyst is expected to possess a wide absorption range for sunlight, high charge separation and transfer efficiencies, strong redox abilities, and good stability, which are theoretically and practically hard to be simultaneously satisfied by one single catalyst (Marschall, 2014). Among the proposed strategies to achieve these requirements, constructing heterojunction photocatalysts, especially in Z-scheme style, has proved to be feasible and efficacious (Low et al., 2017). The two or three components of the Z-scheme structure usually have staggered gaps, and the photogenerated holes (h +) from the lower valence band (VB) of the composite will combine with the electrons (e −) from the lower conduction band (CB), leaving higher VB h + and CB e − for surface redox reactions. Technically, this unique charge migration pattern makes sure that the Z-scheme system can not only maximize its redox abilities, but also gain an improved light absorption and charge separation, which consequently imparts the Z-scheme composite more advantages for degrading the environmental pollutants.

As a metal-free polymeric two-dimensional (2D) material, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, GCN) has received increasing attention in photocatalysis due to its easy synthesis, excellent thermal and chemical stability, non-toxic property, and high CB potential (∼−1.07 eV) (Zhang et al., 2019; Hasija et al., 2021). Meanwhile, its photocatalytic activity remains restricted by relatively large band gap energy (E
g ≈ 2.7 eV) and intrinsically fast recombination of photoexcited charges (Balakrishnan et al., 2023; Kumar et al., 2021). Similarly, another promising visible light responsive catalyst, monoclinic bismuth vanadate (BiVO4, BVO) also has the drawback of fast charge recombination, but the adequate VB potential provides BVO with strong oxidative ability (Zhu et al., 2018). In terms of the matched and favored band edge positions, GCN and BVO are expected as good candidates to construct a Z-scheme composite and take charge of the reduction and oxidation reactions individually (Hu et al., 2022). Moreover, GCN with 2D layer structure can be a flexible support to encapsulate BVO, which facilitates the formation of essentially needed intimate contact between Z-scheme components.

The interfacial resistance at the contact semiconductors may limit the electron transfer (Jiang et al., 2019). The introduction of electron mediators has demonstrated to be a reliable way to promote the electron transfer of a composite in Z-scheme pattern (Liang et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2019). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, have unique near-infrared light absorption, superior electron transfer and storage properties (Tang et al., 2019). Meanwhile, their conjugated π structure and abundant surface functional groups are beneficial to their assembly with other semiconductors (Lu et al., 2016). Owing to these merits, CQDs can be possibly used as electron mediators to improve the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts. For example, with the assistance of CQDs, the CQD-SnNb2O6/BiOCl composite showed the significantly enhanced removal efficiency of benzocaine (Jiang et al., 2019). In contrast with ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3, N-doped CQDs (NCDs) modified composite (ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3) incurred an accelerated photocatalytic degradation of levofloxacin (Li et al., 2020b). However, as far as we know, rare study was reported about the construction of Z-scheme photocatalysts using GCN, CQDs, and BVO. The function of CQDs as electron mediators in the constructed Z-scheme composite also needed an in-depth investigation.

Herein, we designed and fabricated a CQDs-mediated GCN-CQDs/BVO Z-scheme composite via an initial assembly of CQDs with GCN and a followed combination with BVO during its hydrothermal synthesis process. Benzyl paraben (BzP), a typical preservative with higher toxicities than other common Parabens (Bazin et al., 2010; Dobbins et al., 2009), was degraded over these as-synthesized catalysts under visible light irradiation. The effects of various environmental parameters on BzP degradation, and the stability and reusability of the GCN-CQDs/BVO photocatalyst were systematically explored. Furthermore, trapping experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) characterization, and the generation rate estimation of main active species were carried out to reveal the possible photocatalytic mechanism and role of CQDs. In case of the appearance of more toxic intermediates, the acute toxicities of degradation solutions were assayed by exposure of zebrafish embryos. Overall, this work developed an efficient CQDs mediated Z-scheme composite, and explored the roles of CQDs in mediating the electron transfer and promoting the photocatalytic activities thoroughly. Meanwhile, the abatement of the Paraben pollutants as well as their eco-toxicities by photocatalysis was showcased with a deep and systematic study.

Preparation of GCN: GCN was prepared by thermal pyrolysis of urea at 550 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min in a muffle furnace. The obtained GCN was grounded into powder before use. The information of all used chemicals was provided in Supporting Information.

Preparation of CQDs: CQDs were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method based on a previous study (Zhu et al., 2013). Briefly, 1.05 g citric acid monohydrate and 0.5 mL ethylenediamine were added into 10 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min. The mixed solution was then transferred into a PTFE lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the as-formed brownish-black liquid was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was taken as CQDs solution.

Preparation of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite: A simple flow chart of the GCN-CQDs/BVO synthesis is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. For the preparation of CQDs/GCN, 150 μL CQDs suspension was added into 100 mL GCN solution (2 g/L), and stirred for 1 h. After that, the precipitates were washed with ultrapure water and ethanol by centrifuging, and further dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained particles were donated as CQDs/GCN.

For hydrothermal synthesis of GCN-CQDs/BVO composites, 5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.2 g CQDs/GCN were firstly dissolved in 2 mL nitric acid, which was replenished with 7.5 mL ultrapure water to prepare solution A. 5 mmol NH4VO3 and 1 g EDTA were orderly added into 10 mL NaOH (4 M) under stirring to prepare solution B. After 1 h sonication of solution A, solution B was slowly dropped into solution A to form a yellow mixture, which was adjusted to pH 7, transferred to a PTFE lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 18 h. When the reaction completed and cooled, the solution was centrifuged and washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol. The collected precipitates were then dried at 60 °C for 12 h and referred to GCN-CQDs/BVO composite. For comparison, GCN/BVO was also synthesized in a similar way by adding GCN instead of CQDs/GCN into solution A. Without adding GCN or CQDs/GCN, the obtained particles were regarded as pure BVO.

The morphology of the materials was investigated by field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (ZEISS GeminiSEM 300), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was operated on Bruke D8 Advance (40 kV, 40 mA) at a scan rate of 5°/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) and valence band XPS (VB-XPS) analysis were conducted on ThermoFischer ESCALAB 250Xi, where the binding energies were calibrated with C1s at 284.8 eV. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were determined on Varian Cary UV–vis spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were recorded by Hitachi (F-700) spectrofluorophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were captured by Nicolet-6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Raman spectra were measured by a DXR Raman microscopy (Thermo Fisher, USA). EPR spectra were collected by a Bruker EMXnano (Germany) spectrometer using DMPO as the spin-trapping reagent in water or methanol.

Photocatalytic degradation of BzP was carried out in a jacket reactor with a circulating water system. A 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300) was used as the light source, which was fitted with a 420 nm cutoff filter in front. Prior to irradiation, 0.1 g photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL BzP solution (10 mg/L, pH 7) and stirred for 1 h in the dark to reach an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. During the photocatalytic process, 3 mL suspension was sampled every 15 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane to remove the particles. The filtrate was then measured at 256 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (AOE Instruments, A590). The photocatalytic degradation of BzP was expressed as C/C 0, where C was the BzP concentration during the degradation process and C 0 was its initial concentration. The kinetic rate constant K was assessed by equation K = Ln (C
t
/C 0).

Considering the complexity of the real environment, the impacts of several conditions on BzP degradation were explored. The initial photocatalytic solution pH (4, 7, and 10) were prepared by using dilute HCl or NaOH. 5 mM NaNO3, Na2CO3, NaCl, Na2SO4, KCl, CaCl2, and MgCl2 were employed to investigate the effects of distinct coexisting ion. Humic acid (HA) at concentrations of 0.5 and 1 mM was chosen to investigate the effect of natural organic matters. Considering the influence of HA on the absorbance of BzP, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Elite, P3100, Dalian) equipped with a reversed-phase C18 column (Zorbax SB-Aq, Agilent, USA) and a UV–Vis detector was used to determine the concentration of BzP at 256 nm. The mobile phase was the mixture of 30% water and 70% methanol. The flow rate was set at 0.8 mL/min.

To test the stability and reusability of the photocatalyst, a five consecutive experiment was carried out. The same photocatalyst was collected after each reaction run, washed with ultrapure water and ethanol, dried, and reused for the next run.

To determine the active species generated in photocatalytic BzP degradation process, 0.3 mM benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and ammonium oxalate (AO) were used respectively as the superoxide radicals (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and h + scavengers under the similar photocatalytic conditions. Since BQ would induce the colour change of the BzP solution, HPLC with the UV–Vis detector was used to quantify the BzP. The analysis condition was same with the case of HA. The production of •OH was further assessed by terephthalic (TA) fluorescence method (Gong and Teoh, 2015). Specifically, 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL TA solution (1 mM, pH 12), and exposed to light irradiation for 15 min 1 mL suspension was then withdrawn every 2 min, filtered, and analysed by fluorescence spectrophotometry (Infinite M Nano, Tecan) with excitation at 315 nm 100 μM 2,3-bis(2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophehyl)2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide (XTT) was used to determine the •O2 − generation of different photocatalysts (1 g/L) (Li et al., 2013). In photocatalytic process, 3 mL of the reaction suspension was taken out at given time intervals and filtered. The concentration of the orange XTT-formazan produced from the reduction of XTT by •O2 − was measured using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 476 nm.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements of the as-prepared photocatalysts were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument 660D) using a standard three-electrode system with Ag/AgCl and Pt as reference electrode and counter electrode. The working electrode was prepared by drop-casting the photocatalyst (1 g/L) suspension containing 0.5% Nafion onto the pre-cleaned fluorine-doped tin oxide conductive glass (FTO) at a loading of 2 mg/cm2. The electrolyte was 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution adjusted to pH 7. A 300 W Xenon lamp was used for the irradiation. According to Nernst equation: E RHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.059 pH + E°Ag/AgCl (0.197 V), PEC potential was converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale (at pH 0). The photocurrent response was measured at +1.23 V vs RHE with the switched light on and off every 20 s. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured using an amplitude perturbation of 5 mV between 100 kHz and 50 mHz. Mott-Schottky analysis was performed at 1000 Hz in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity of BzP degradation solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The acute toxicities of BzP degradation solutions sampled at different time (0, 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 min) were assayed on normally developed zebrafish embryos at 2 h post fertilization (hpf). More specifically, twenty-five embryos were exposed to 5 mL of the filtered reaction solution and cultured in one well of a 6-well plate at 28 ± 0.5 °C with a photoperiod of 14 h light and 10 h dark. Each group contained three replicate wells (n = 3). The same volume of Milli-Q water was set as control. The reaction solutions and Milli-Q water were renewed every day to avoid the evaporation. The development of zebrafish embryos was observed at certain period of time (12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf), during which the mortality and hatchability were recorded. At 96 hpf of culture, zebrafish larvae that developed edema and spine curvature were regarded as malformed and counted. Heart rate of the larvae (n = 10) was counted directly under a microscope at 96 hpf.

The fabrication process of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite is illustrated in Fig. 1a. The morphologies of the as-prepared materials were characterized by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. As can be seen from Fig. S1, pristine GCN exhibited an irregular sheet-like structure. TEM image confirmed that the lamellate GCN comprised of ultrathin nanosheets with many pores, which provides large surface to contact with other material (Fig. 1b). Pristine BVO displayed the regular decahedron structure (Fig. 1c). CQDs nanoparticles were noticed to be slightly aggregated and mostly in diameter below 10 nm (Fig. 1d). The identified lattice fringes with spacing of 0.22 nm corresponded to (100) planes of their graphite carbons (Fig. 1e) (Zhou et al., 2022). In TEM image of the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite (Fig. 1f), the top dark area (box 1) whose fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern matched well with (121) planes of monoclinic BVO, was wrapped with a thin GCN layer at lattice spacing of 0.33 nm (002). Benefited from the initial assembly, CQDs were mainly distributed on the ultrathin GCN layer, which basically was the interface of GCN and BVO. Hence, the as-formed intimate contact among GCN, CQDs, and BVO was anticipated to favor the interfacial charge transfer, and further lead to an enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The crystalline structures of the photocatalysts were investigated by XRD. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, GCN had two diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.6°, which could be indexed to (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4 (JCPDS 87–1526) and contributed by in-plane packing of tri-s-triazine units and interlayer stacking of aromatic structures, respectively (Ong et al., 2016). In the diffraction spectrum of CQDs, only one broad peak was observed at 23.1° and assigned to (100) planes of its graphite constituents (Li et al., 2019). For BVO, its diffraction pattern correlated well with the monoclinic scheelite BiVO4 (JCPDS NO. 14–0688). GCN-CQDs/BVO displayed a similar XRD pattern with BVO, but a GCN diffraction peak was noted and shifted to 27.9° (Fig. 2b), which might be caused by the contact of CQDs, GCN, and BVO through the designed heterojunction vicinity (Zhu et al., 2020). The undetected diffraction peak of CQDs in GCN-CQDs/BVO was possibly due to its small amounts, high dispersion, and/or low crystallinity (Liang et al., 2020).

The chemical composition of the as-synthesized materials was further identified by Raman and FTIR spectroscopy. Instead of a distinct D or G band, a broad peak at 760-2300 cm−1 was shown in Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. S2), possibly resulted from its high fluorescence (Zhu et al., 2013). BVO contained two peaks at 710 and 831 cm−1, ascribed to the related asymmetric and symmetric V–O stretching modes (Wetchakun et al., 2012). After incorporation of CQDs/GCN with BVO, the intensities of the two BVO peaks significantly decreased as the broad CQDs peak appeared, corroborating the successful formation of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite. Meanwhile, the characteristic IR peaks belonging to GCN (1200-1700 cm−1), CQDs (1048 cm−1), and BVO (746 cm−1) were all shown in the spectrum of GCN-CQDs/BVO (Fig. S3), which evidenced their existence in the ternary composite additionally. The detailed IR information about these peaks is interpreted in Supporting Information.

XPS analysis was employed to probe the surface chemical states of the photocatalysts. Compared to that of pure GCN, CQDs, and BVO, the survey spectrum of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite contained the expected elements of C, N, Bi, V, and O (Fig. 3 a). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CQDs elucidated three peaks centered at 284.6 eV (C–C/CC), 286.1 eV (C–NH2/C–O), and 287.7 eV (CO) (Fig. 3b) (Hu et al., 2021), while C 1s of GCN was deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.6 eV (C–C/CC) and 288.1 eV (N–CN) (Liu et al., 2019a). The presence of C–C/CC, C–NH2/C–O, and N–CN were all observed in C 1s spectrum of GCN-CQDs/BVO, verifying the sourced C from GCN and CQDs. Fig. 3c illustrated the N 1s spectra of CQDs, GCN, and GCN-CQDs/BVO. The peaks at 399.7 and 401 eV of CQDs were ascribed to the N 1s in tertiary (N-(C)3) bonding and amino groups (-NH2), respectively (Zhang et al., 2016). Besides these two peaks, N 1s of GCN showed additional two peaks at 398.5 and 404.5 eV, which corresponded to sp2 hybridized N atoms (C–NC) and charging effect in triazine-based ring (Xia et al., 2017). The N 1s pattern of GCN-CQDs/BVO revealed that the dominated N species in the composite were similar to these in GCN. Pure BVO presented two characteristic Bi3+ peaks at 158.3 and 163.6 eV in Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 3d), and two V5+ peaks at 516 and 523.4 eV in V 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e). Notably, these Bi3+ and V5+ peaks shifted toward higher binding energies in spectra of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite. In addition, O 1s spectra were depicted in Fig. 3f. As can be seen, CQDs consisted of two O 1s peaks at 530.9 and 532.1 eV, which were related to C–O and CO (Zhang et al., 2016). The O 1s spectrum of pure BVO was decomposed into lattice oxygen located at 529.1 eV and the oxygen in surface hydroxyl groups at 531.2 eV (Jiang et al., 2022). By comparison, the O 1s spectrum of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite showed three O 1s peaks belonging to lattice oxygen, surface hydroxyl groups, and CO with slight shift to higher values. Here, the observed positive shift of Bi 4f, V 2p and O 1s peaks in GCN-CQDs/BVO composite suggested the possible interfacial electrons transfer from BVO to GCN via CQDs mediators, and the consequently resulted less electrons accumulation on BVO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band structure and photogenerated charge transfer
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties and energy band features of the materials were examined by DRS. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, both GCN and BVO possessed visible light absorption, signifying their applications in solar energy field. By comparison, the absorption range of GCN was remarkably extended after CQDs loading. This phenomenon is also reflected in the comparison of GCN/BVO and GCN-CQDs/BVO, demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs could efficiently improve the light absorption, and consequently facilitate the increase in photocatalytic activities of the catalysts. Additionally, the band gaps of pristine GCN and BVO were evaluated to be 2.36 and 2.71 eV (Fig. 4b), which were consistent with previous report (Jiang et al., 2017). Meanwhile, the VB potentials of GCN and BVO were determined to be at approximate +1.47 and + 2.4 eV from their VB-XPS spectra (Fig. 4c). Based on the equation of E
CB = E
VB - Eg, the CB potentials were then calculated to be −1.24 eV for GCN and 0.04 eV for BVO, which were close to their Mott-Schottky results, that was, −1.14 and 0 eV, respectively (Fig. S4). As a result, the photogenerated e
- on CB of GCN was capable to reduce O2 to •O2 − (E o (O2/•O2 −) = −0.33 eV), and h + on VB of BVO was able to oxidize OH− to produce •OH (E o (•OH/OH−) = +2.1 eV) (Wang et al., 2018). A clear view about the band structures of GCN and BVO is schemed in Fig. 4d, which provides the powerful support for GCN and BVO Z-scheme engineering.

Transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance tests were conducted to characterize the charge separation of the materials. Due to the inherently low separation efficiency of photogenerated e
- -h + pairs, pristine GCN and BVO had weak photocurrent responses (Fig. 4e). The noted transient current spikes might be caused by the accumulated electrons in the electrodes, and the resultant recombination with holes. Concurrently, the large semicircle arc in Nyquist plots of GCN and BVO also indicated their relatively high interfacial charge transfer resistance (Fig. 4f). Although coupling with CQDs did not improve the photocurrent response of GCN pronouncedly, the reduced semicircle arc of CQDs/GCN as compared to pure GCN implies the minimized charge transfer resistance by CQDs. The photocurrent intensity of GCN/BVO was noticeably higher than that of GCN, BVO, as well as CQDs/GCN, as resulted from the matched band structures of GCN and BVO, and hereby improved charge separation and transfer at the GCN/BVO heterojunction. The interfacial charge transfer resistance of GCN/BVO was also reduced prominently, displaying a smaller semicircle arc in Nyquist plot. With the assistance of CQDs, GCN-CQDs/BVO composite generated the highest photocurrent intensity and smallest arc radius, reflecting the further promoted charge separation by CQDs.

The effects of GCN/BVO heterojunction and CQDs incorporation on photocharge separation were also demonstrated in PL analysis. Generally speaking, a weaker PL intensity designates a lower charge recombination rate and faster charge separation. In contrast with pristine GCN and BVO, GCN/BVO possessed a substantially reduced PL intensity (Fig. S5), suggesting the inhibited photocharge recombination. With CQDs additionally promoted charge transfer, GCN-CQDs/BVO exhibited the lowest PL intensity. Based on these results, it can be inferred that both the GCN/BVO heterojunction and CQDs mediation play significant roles in accelerating the charge separation of the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of BzP by prepared catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of BzP by as-prepared catalysts were carried out under visible light irradiation. Fig. 5 a showed that BzP was negligibly degraded without the addition of catalyst, indicating its ignorable direct photolysis. Pure GCN and BVO generated the relatively low photocatalytic activities, reaching only 24.7% and 47.7% BzP degraded in 150 min. After loading CQDs on GCN, the degradation efficiency of BzP was nearly doubled as relative to pure GCN, which should be benefited from the effective electron transfer of CQDs that enhanced the production of •O2 − on GCN (Jin et al., 2021), as well as the significantly increased light absorption (Fig. 4a). In particular, the binary composite GCN/BVO showed a more superior photocatalytic BzP degradation (79.5%) than these of pure GCN and BVO because of the efficiently enhanced charge separation. With further inhibition of the charge recombination, the CQDs mediated GCN-CQDs/BVO composite exhibited the optimal photocatalytic degradation of BzP (85.7%). Moreover, the pseudo-first-order rates (K) of BzP degradation on GCN, BVO, CQDs/GCN, GCN/BVO, and GCN-CQDs/BVO photocatalysts were calculated to be 0.00169, 0.00387, 0.00459, 0.01083, and 0.01221 min−1, respectively (Fig. 5b). Obviously, the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite had the highest degradation rate, which was 7, 3, and 1.13-fold greater than that of GCN, BVO, and GCN/BVO. These results are consistent with PEC analysis, further emphasizing the critical roles of the constructed GCN-CQDs/BVO junction and CQDs mediating effect. The optimization of CQDs and CQDs/GCN amounts in GCN-CQDs/BVO is detailed in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7.

In view of the complexity of real environmental waters, the effects of pH value, coexisting ions, and natural organic matters on BzP degradation over GCN-CQDs/BVO were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5c, the degradation efficiency of BzP reached the optimum at neutral pH. Either high alkalinity (pH 10) or acidity (pH 4) negatively impacted the BzP degradation. As an important parameter of water samples, pH could affect the protonation-deprotonation reaction of organic compounds and surface charges of photocatalyst. More specifically, the dissociation constant (pKa) of BzP was 8.33 (Xu et al., 2021), which meant that BzP would form the deprotonated species at pH > 8.33. Meanwhile, Zeta potential test elucidated that the surface of GCN-CQDs/BVO composite would be negatively charged at pH > 4.3 (Fig. S8). Therefore, electrostatic repulsion might exist between GCN-CQDs/BVO and deprotonated BzP when pH of the reaction solution was at 10, which thereby inhibited its degradation. Under acidic condition (pH 4), H+ could not only consume •O2 − (H+ + •O2 − → HOO•), but also harm the formation of •OH, consequently resulting in the decreased BzP degradation as compared to neutral pH (Xing et al., 2020). Fig. 5d reveals the effects of coexisting anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) on BzP degradation by GCN-CQDs/BVO. During photocatalytic process, the existence of Cl− may scavenge h + and •OH to form chlorine radical (Cl•). When the concentration of Cl− was low (5 mM), the generation of Cl• might offset the effect of •OH consuming to some extent, and incur the negligible impact to the BzP degradation (Liang et al., 2020). A moderate suppression occurred in the presence of CO3 2−, possibly caused by its higher alkalinity (Jiang et al., 2019). In contrast, the addition of SO4 2− induced a more obvious inhibition of BzP degradation, which was assumed that SO4 2− reacted with h + and •OH, forming less reactive •SO4 2− (Hu et al., 2003). The addition of NO3 − might consume photogenerated electrons, also resulting in the inhibition of BzP degradation (Chen et al., 2016b). The effects of cations on photodegradation of BzP are shown in Fig. 5e. Among the studied cations, Na+ had a slight effect, while K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ reduced the BzP degradation with increasing efficacy. Considering the negatively charged GCN-CQDs/BVO at neutral pH (Fig. S8), the competitive adsorption between these cations and BzP might appear and closely relate with the radius and positive charge amount of the cation (Chen et al., 2016a), which therefore restrained the BzP degradation. Humic acid (HA) was used as the representative natural organic matter in this study. As shown in Fig. 5f, the degradation efficiency of BzP significantly decreased with the addition of HA (0.5 mM). Especially, with further increasing the HA concentration to 1 mM, the impact of inhibition increased, which was attributed to the fact that HA could not only scavenge the active species, but also compete for light absorption (Carlos et al., 2012).

To evaluate the recyclability and stability of the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite, five consecutive experiments were carried out by adding the same catalyst in each cycle. As showed in Fig. S9a, GCN-CQDs/BVO only retained 70% of its initial photocatalytic activity after five cycled runs. XRD diffraction pattern of the cycled sample was similar to that recorded before the reaction, suggesting the unchanged crystal structure of the composite (Fig. S9a). Therefore, the reduced photocatalytic activity may be attributed to the catalyst loss in each cycle, adsorption of the remaining BzP and intermediates on the photocatalyst surface (Lv et al., 2020).

Active species play the vital and indispensable roles in photocatalytic process. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the addition of AO, IPA, and BQ as the specific trapping agents for h +, •OH, and •O2 − inhibited the BzP degradation by 13.6%, 29.3%, and 43.9%, respectively, which suggested that •O2 − and •OH were the dominant active species and h + contributed comparatively little to BzP degradation by GCN-CQDs/BVO. The formation of •O2 − and •OH was further confirmed by in situ EPR technique. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, no EPR signal could be observed in dark, whereas the characteristic signals for DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − were well observed under visible light irradiation, verifying their generation in GCN-CQDs/BVO photocatalytic system sufficiently.

To shed more light on the origin of CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of GCN-CQDs/BVO, the generation of •OH and •O2 − was additionally assessed by using TA and XTT as indicators. The method of TA assay is based on the nucleophilic attack by •OH on aromatic ring of TA to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which emits strong fluorescence at 425 nm (inset of Fig. 6c) (Gong and Teoh, 2015). XTT assay is based on its reduction by •O2 −, which forms the orange XTT-formazan with maximum absorption at 476 nm (inset of Fig. 6d) (Li et al., 2013). The results showed that GCN-CQDs/BVO composite could convert TA to TAOH as a function of the irradiation time, and the fitted generation rate of •OH was nearly six times higher than that of GCN/BVO (Fig. 6c). The reduction of XTT by GCN-CQDs/BVO was also more noticeable than that of GCN/BVO, demonstrating the faster generation of •O2 − (Fig. 6d). The comparison of •OH and •O2 − generation between GCN-CQDs/BVO and GCN/BVO evidenced the strong effect of CQDs as electron mediators, which promoted the charge separation and thereby induced more active species involved in photocatalytic reaction.

Based on above results, the mechanism of GCN-CQDs/BVO photocatalytic system was proposed and displayed in Fig. 6e. Under visible light irradiation, both GCN and BVO were excited, and photogenerated e − and h + located on their CB and VB, respectively. Due to the superior electron transfer and storage abilities, CQDs acted as electron mediators which transferred the e − on CB of BVO to combine with h + on VB of GCN, leaving the accumulated h + on VB of BVO and e − on CB of GCN. Owing to the more negative CB potential (−1.24 eV) than O2/•O2 − reduction (−0.33 eV), the photoexcited e − on GCN could readily capture O2 to form •O2 −, while h + on BVO were consumed to produce •OH as its VB potential (+2.4 eV) was more positive than that of •OH/OH− (+2.1 eV) (Fig. 4b). Consequently, the CQDs mediated GCN and BVO Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed, endowing the maintained high redox ability of the system. In this case, due to the lower VB potential of GCN (+1.47 eV) than that of E
o (•OH/OH−) and higher CB potential of BVO (+0.04 eV) than that of E
o (O2/•O2 −), the migration of e − and h + only in Z-scheme could engender the generation of the detected •OH and •O2 − in above trapping experiments. Although direct Z-scheme system might exist between GCN and BVO (Hu et al., 2022b; Lu et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2019), with CQDs mediated electron transfer, the charge separation of GCN-CQDs/BVO Z-scheme composite could be further significantly enhanced as compared to GCN/BVO.

Among the commonly used Paraben preservatives, BzP was reported to elicit the relatively higher acute and subchronic toxicities to organisms (Bazin et al., 2010; Dobbins et al., 2009). Therefore, the acute toxicity towards zebrafish embryos (2 hpf) was assessed for BzP and its filtered degradation solutions, aiming to examine the toxicity transition alongside the photocatalytic degradation. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the initial BzP solution without degradation (0 min) caused 10.9% mortality of the embryos after exposure for 12 hpf, and rapidly increased to 63.9% at 24 hpf. Although no obvious increase was found further, the significantly high mortality of the embryos relative to the control group demonstrated the potent acute toxicity of BzP to aquatic organisms. Comparatively, the degradation solutions showed a gradually decreasing mortality as a function of the degradation time. In particular, after being degraded for 90 min, the reaction solutions induced non-significant mortality, implying the remarkably decreased toxicity of BzP by photocatalytic degradation. Meanwhile, the survived embryos in BzP exposure group (0 min) could not hatch till 96 hpf (Fig. 7b). Although all degradation solutions caused lower hatching rates at 48 hpf than that of the control group, there was no significant difference at 96 hpf. These findings indicated that the BzP degradation products could lead to a delayed hatching, but the delay effect was attenuated with the extension of degradation time. At 96 hpf, despite that a more severe malformation (inset of Fig. 7c) was observed for the larvae hatched in the solutions degraded at 30 and 60 min, no significant difference was found between the sampled solutions at 90, 120, and 150 min and the control group (Fig. 7c). Similar trend was illustrated with regard to the heart rate (Fig. 7d). Overall, these results provide compelling evidence that BzP imposed potent toxicity to aquatic organisms, while photocatalytic degradation could effectively remove BzP, in concordance with the significantly lowered toxicities.

In this work, a Z-scheme GCN-CQDs/BVO composite was synthesized, and CQDs located dominantly at the intimate interface of GCN and BVO. By virtue of the proper band energy alignment and CQDs mediated electron transfer, the as-constructed composite exhibited the significantly enhanced charge separation and also maintained high redox ability, which thereby degraded the BzP under visible light irradiation efficiently. A neutral pH was more proper for BzP degradation over the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite, while other environmental parameters, such as coexisting cations and anions, natural organic matters, could interfere the BzP degradation to some degree. With good stability and reusability for practical application, the GCN-CQDs/BVO composite could also reduce the strong toxicity of BzP during its photocatalytic degradation process. In general, this study provides a valuable strategy for design and synthesis of efficient Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts to practically remove organic pollutants in aqueous media while reducing their eco-toxicities.
Maosheng Tian: Methodology; Chenyan Hu: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft; Junxia Yu: Methodology; Lianguo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.
